1:
The chapter opens on two puzzles, and the witnesses spend their strength on both: why God's anger burned against Israel, and in what sense he moved David to the sin that followed. The reformed Gill sets the historical frame, hearing an echo of the famine that had already fallen for Saul's blood-guilt against the Gibeonites (2 Samuel 21:1): the anger, kindled once, now breaks out afresh — whether for some secret national sin, or for the multitudes who had followed Absalom and then Sheba in rebellion, though the text names no cause.

The grammatical-historical commentators refuse to fasten the wrath on the census itself. Ellicott insists the anger was kindled not by the numbering but by what led to it — the rising spirit of "earthly pride and reliance on earthly strength" in both king and people; and he does the philology, noting that the verb rendered number in this verse is not the military-muster word used through the rest of the chapter, but simply "to count." Keil and Delitzsch survey the whole movement that follows — the census Joab dissuades, the seer Gad, the choice of three judgments, the pestilence that slew seventy thousand — treating this verse as the seed of it all.

The great crux is the clause he moved David. Here the historian says the Lord moved him, while the parallel in 1 Chronicles 21:1 says Satan provoked him, and the witnesses labour to hold the two together. Ellicott calls it "a striking instance of attributing directly to God whatever comes about under His permission," yet presses further: God has established immutable spiritual laws that are but the expression of his unchanging will, so that David's pride, ripening of itself, is said to be God's doing because God had so ordered that such a sinful act should be the natural fruit of such a sinful state. The wesleyan Clarke reads the same seam biographically: David, "slackening in his piety and confidence toward God," was meditating some extension of his dominions and grew curious to count his fighting men; God could not be angry with him for numbering had he simply moved him to it, so Satan is the tempter.

The puritan Matthew Henry weighs six theories of where the evil lay — numbering those under twenty, omitting the half-shekel ransom (Exodus 30:12), a design to tax, acting without God's order, an affront to the promise that Abraham's seed should be innumerable, and, worst, the pride of the heart. He settles the paradox as the others do: "God is not the author of sin; he tempts no man"; Satan suggested it as sin, as he put it into Judas's heart to betray Christ, while God as righteous Judge permitted it, that from David's sin he might take occasion to punish Israel for their own. What seems to us a small offence "may be a great sin in the eye of God, who ... is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart."
2:
The order itself is brief — "number ye the people, that I may know the number of the people" — but the witnesses find in that bare command both a geography and a motive. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the scope of the muster in the proverbial formula from Dan even to Beersheba, the standing phrase for the whole land from its northern to its southern limit (compare Judges 20:1), and they gloss the verb pakad as a military reckoning, the same term used when Moses mustered the tribes for war (Numbers 1:44). It is not, then, a mere counting of heads but a survey of fighting strength.

Keil and Delitzsch linger, too, over a textual wrinkle. The Hebrew here reads that Joab was "with him" — the captain of the host who stood at the king's side — where the parallel in Chronicles reads instead "and to the rulers of the people." They defend the received text against emendation, judging the Chronicler's phrase a later simplification drawn from the fuller narrative below, and refusing to let the versions smooth away a reading that all the ancient translators but one preserve.

The reformed Gill adds only that Joab, "the captain of the host," was now at court, a counsellor as well as a general, so that the commission fell to the one man best placed to carry it through the length of the kingdom. Between them the two witnesses leave the verse's weight where the historian sets it — on the little clause that I may know the number. There is the whole disorder of the enterprise: not a duty laid on David, not a need pressed by any danger, but a wish to know, for knowing's sake, how great a host he commanded. The pride diagnosed in the previous verse now speaks its own purpose aloud, and the reader who has been told the anger of the Lord was already kindled watches the king walk, unbidden, straight into the occasion of it. What is asked is lawful in itself — Moses had numbered Israel more than once — yet asked here in the pride of the heart, and asked of a people whose increase was God's blessing and not the king's achievement, it becomes the hinge on which the whole tragedy turns.
3:
Joab's protest is the surprise of the chapter, and every witness marks it: the rough soldier, no friend of scruples, becomes for once the voice of conscience against his king. The reformed Gill notes the manner as much as the matter — Joab speaks now "in a more modest, decent, and polite manner" than when he had rated David harshly for mourning Absalom (compare 2 Samuel 19:5); the remonstrance is respectful, but it is a remonstrance still.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the shrewdness behind it. Keil and Delitzsch hear Joab wishing the people multiplied a hundredfold and the king's own eyes spared to see it, yet asking, "why doth my lord the king delight in this thing?" — for he saw through the intention, perceived that the census could bring no real advantage to the government and might breed dissatisfaction among the people. Ellicott presses the point harder: even in the eyes of the unscrupulous Joab, a man who "never gives evidence of being influenced by religious motives," David's act was abominable, at variance with the very principle of the national existence; and he records the sharper words the Chronicler preserves — "why will he be a cause of trespass to Israel?" The objection, Ellicott adds, was sustained by the subordinate captains, so that David carried the thing through "by sheer force of self-will."

The wesleyan Clarke states it most bluntly of all. This "very bad man saw that the measure now recommended by the king was a wrong one, and might be ruinous to the people," and so he reasoned against it in a sensible speech; "but the king was infatuated, and would hear no reason." The witnesses do not divide here; they converge on a single, uncomfortable lesson. When the counsel of a hardened man of war is wiser and more godly than the resolve of the sweet singer of Israel, the fault lies not in the counsel but in the will that overrides it. That Joab, of all men, should plead for restraint, and that David, of all men, should refuse to hear him, measures how far pride had unseated the king's judgment. The reader is left to note that God often sets an unlikely monitor in the proud man's path, and that to silence such a voice by rank rather than answer it by reason is itself a further step into the sin.
4:
This verse records the collapse of the protest — "the king's word prevailed against Joab, and against the captains of the host" — and the witnesses read it as the tragedy of authority misused. The reformed Gill observes that the captains were of Joab's mind, all against the numbering, yet "their arguments and remonstrances were of no avail with the king"; he was determined, and laid his commands upon them, which they were bound to obey. The word that had counselled in vain must now execute what it had condemned.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch turn to the mechanics of the muster. Joab and the captains "went out from the presence of the king to number the people," and the historian notes that they encamped — pitching their headquarters in the open country because such crowds assembled to be counted. Keil and Delitzsch fix the starting-point at Aroer, distinguished from the Aroer of the Arnon in Reuben's territory (compare Joshua 13:25), the town in the valley of Gad before Rabbah; and though the notice of encampment is given only for the first station, they judge it meant to apply to the others as well.

The two witnesses together leave the verse as a study in the machinery of a wrong once set in motion. Joab has said his piece and been overruled; there is no more arguing, only the long circuit of the land to be walked and the tally to be kept. It is worth marking, with Gill, that obedience here is no vindication of the command: the captains do the king's bidding because to refuse would be read as grudging their labour in his service, not because they think the thing right. So a whole nation is set in motion by one proud word, and the reluctance of the men who carry it out only deepens the pathos. The verse says nothing of God, and that silence is itself eloquent — for nine months and more the enterprise will grind forward under heaven's patience before the reckoning falls, and the king who would not hear his general will presently have to hear his seer.
5:
With the surveyors across the Jordan the narrative becomes a march, and the witnesses turn cartographer, tracing the long circuit the census took. The grammatical-historical Ellicott lays out the whole route in advance: the count began on the east of Jordan at the extreme south, passed northward through the eastern tribes, then crossed the river and moved southward through the western tribes — a great sweeping loop around the kingdom. Its first station, Aroer, he identifies as the city "on the river Arnon" described at the southern border of the trans-Jordanic territory (compare Deuteronomy 2:36).

Ellicott also touches the text's difficulties before they thicken. He prefers to read a full stop after the word river, and to render the next phrase "towards Gad" rather than "of Gad," suspecting that the words "and they came" have dropped from the text; Jazer he names as a boundary city of Gad (compare Joshua 13:25), whence the party turned to Gilead. Keil and Delitzsch confess themselves beaten by the same terrain: the phrase that follows Aroer was already unintelligible to the ancient translators, who guessed variously, and they judge the received wording corrupt beyond recovery, declining the emendations others have proposed.

The reformed Gill keeps the theological thread running under the geography, noting that the crossing of Jordan was made "to take the number of the tribes of Reuben and Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh first." That small observation is worth its place: the historian is not merely mapping, he is showing us the thoroughness of the thing, the king's men beginning at the very edge of the promised inheritance and working their patient way in. What reads on the surface as a dry itinerary is, in the flow of the chapter, the slow unfolding of the sin — every town entered, every fighting man tallied, is one more stroke of the pen that Joab warned against. The obscurity of the place-names, which so exercises the commentators, only underscores how remote and total the reckoning was: to the farthest, half-known corners of the land the count reached, that David might know his strength, and that his strength might become his snare.
6:
The march presses on to the northern marches of the land, and the witnesses divide their labour between naming the ground and mending the text. The wesleyan Clarke does the geography with relish. The obscure "land of Tahtim-hodshi" he takes, from the margin, as a "nether land newly inhabited" — perhaps the country east of Gilead that Israel had conquered from the Hagarites (compare 1 Chronicles 5:10). Dan-jaan he renders "Dan of the woods," the northernmost city of the whole inheritance, at the foot of Mount Lebanon near the springs of Jordan, as Beersheba was the southernmost — whence, he reminds us, the proverbial "from Dan to Beersheba" for the length of the land.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott treats the same names more warily. "Tahtim-hodshi," of which the ancient versions make nothing, he judges a corruption, conjecturing that "Tahtim" should be read "Hittites" — a change of a single letter — and that "Hodshi" is the remnant of a phrase naming the month of the surveyors' arrival. Dan-jaan he too allows may be "Dan in the forest," following the Greek and the Vulgate. As for Zidon, and Tyre after it, he notes the awkward fact that these Phoenician mother-cities, though reckoned nominally to Asher, were never actually possessed by Israel; the census-takers either came only to their confines or, being on friendly terms, entered to enumerate the Israelites dwelling there.

The reformed Gill, characteristically, keeps the count's logic in view, observing that the land of Gilead — half given to Reuben and Gad, half to the half-tribe of Manasseh — was numbered "the first of all." Between them the witnesses turn a tangle of half-legible names into a coherent picture: the king's men sweeping up the eastern side of the land to its very roof, brushing the borders of great heathen cities that were Israel's only in name. That the text has grown so uncertain at just these far edges is itself a kind of parable — the reckoning reached to the dim frontier, gathering in strength that God had given and the king would presently be made to lose. The commentators contend only over letters and locations; on the drift of the passage they are one.
7:
The circuit now turns south and west toward home, and the witnesses close the map and reach for the sum. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the last leg: down to the fortress of Zor, that is Tyre (compare Joshua 19:29), and into "all the towns of the Hivites and Canaanites" — the cities of Naphtali, Zebulun, and Issachar, the region that would later be called Galilee, where the old inhabitants had never been exterminated but only made tributary; thence to the south of Judah, to Beersheba. When the whole land had been traversed the surveyors returned to Jerusalem, and Keil and Delitzsch here record the totals that verse nine will give — eight hundred thousand men of Israel fit to draw the sword, and five hundred thousand of Judah — noting at once the divergence from the Chronicler's figures and ascribing it to the approximate, round character of both reckonings.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott fills in the ethnography. The "Hivites" and "Canaanites" are the surviving remnants of the land's first peoples, still occupying distinct towns of their own; the Hivites lay chiefly in the north, though Gibeon and its towns had been theirs, while "the Canaanites" serves as a general name for the leavings of all the other races. The reformed Gill adds a scruple of his own about Tyre: the "strong hold of Tyre" must mean old Tyre and the parts near it, for the island city itself lay outside the land of Israel, and its inhabitants were not to be numbered.

Taken together the witnesses show the survey completing its great arc and beginning to yield its figure. The very care with which they mark who could and could not properly be counted — the tributary Canaanite towns, the Tyrian border — throws into relief the questionableness of the whole undertaking. Here is a kingdom stretched to its farthest bounds, its manpower gathered to a total, and the reader knows already, from the anger kindled in the first verse and the protest silenced in the third, that this proud arithmetic is being kept only that it may be answered in blood. The historian lets the itinerary run its full length so that the number, when it lands, may fall with all its weight.
8:
The surveyors come home "at the end of nine months and twenty days," and the witnesses seize on the span of time and the trustworthiness of the tally. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch use the long interval to explain the numbers themselves. When the count was handed to the king it stood at eight hundred thousand men of Israel and five hundred thousand of Judah, against the Chronicler's eleven hundred thousand and four hundred and seventy thousand; the difference, they argue, arose because the figures rested on oral tradition and were never entered in the annals of the kingdom (compare 1 Chronicles 27:24). They are approximations in round thousands, not exact rolls, and give no ground for a charge of exaggeration — a total population near five or six millions being no impossibility in so fertile a land.

The wesleyan Clarke fastens on the sheer labour of it. Nine months and twenty days "was a considerable time," yet the work was neither easy nor even fully done (compare 1 Chronicles 21:6). He reaches for a famous parallel — William the Conqueror's survey of all England, the Domesday Book, which took six years to complete, particularizing every hide of land, every mill and fishery, every villein and bondman; and he judges Joab's task the more difficult of the two. The reformed Gill simply follows the route to its close, noting that the party, "beginning at the east, and from thence to the north, and then going about to the west, came to the south, which finished their circuit."

Between them the witnesses make the passage of time itself part of the indictment. Nine months and twenty days is the span in which a child is carried and born; and through all that stretch, while the king's men walked the land counting his strength, the sin lay unrepented and the anger of the Lord waited. Clarke's Domesday comparison quietly sharpens the point: David has done what earthly conquerors do, taken the measure of his realm for his own security and pride, and the very length and thoroughness of the reckoning show how settled the purpose was. The historian, by pausing to date the return so precisely, lets the reader feel how long God's patience ran before the reckoning came due.
9:
Joab lays the total before the king, and the witnesses gather at once around the famous discrepancy of the numbers. The bare figures — eight hundred thousand valiant men in Israel, five hundred thousand in Judah — do not agree with the parallel in 1 Chronicles 21:5, which gives eleven hundred thousand and four hundred and seventy thousand, and the great division among the commentators is how to account for the gap.

The wesleyan Clarke takes the honest, unflinching road of textual criticism. Neither sum is too great, he says, but they cannot both be right, and he confesses it is hard to say which is true; he owns plainly that "more corruptions have taken place in the numbers of the historical books of the Old Testament than in any other part of the sacred records." His remedy is a careful theology of inspiration: the original writers wrote under the Spirit, "but we are not told that the same influence descended on all copiers of their words"; the scribes could err and did err, and a patient collation of the historical books corrects the essential mistakes.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott resists the appeal to corruption. There is no need, he argues, to suppose the text disturbed in either place: Joab "undertook the work unwillingly, and performed it imperfectly," refusing to number Levi and Benjamin (compare 1 Chronicles 21:6), never finishing the count, so that no official record was made and the numbers were in part mere estimates. The higher figure in Chronicles may include the omitted tribes; the differing total for Judah may turn on whether priests, Levites, or a standing army were reckoned in. Keil and Delitzsch stand with him, calling both sets of figures approximate and the divergence a fruit of unrecorded tradition rather than of error.

The debate is real but narrow, and it is bounded by a shared reverence. Whether the gap is a copyist's slip (Clarke) or the natural residue of an incomplete and unofficial census (Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch), none of the witnesses lets the difficulty touch the substance of the history. And Gill, keeping the story moving, points already past the total to what follows: the number given, the sin now fully done, "David's heart smote him," so that the very arithmetic that was meant to feed his pride becomes the occasion of his grief.
10:
The tally is scarcely delivered before conscience strikes, and the witnesses gather about the swiftness and the depth of David's repentance. The puritan Matthew Henry marks the timing with care: through all the nine months of the counting David felt nothing, "but, when the account was finished and laid before him, that very night his conscience was awakened," and the pleasure he had promised himself "was turned into the gall of asps within him." His heart smote him before ever the prophet came — a good sign, Henry says, "of a principle of grace in the heart," for "true penitents, whose consciences are tender and well informed, see that evil in sin which others do not see." David owns not merely that he has sinned but that he has "done foolishly, very foolishly," because he did it in the pride of his heart, glorying in the numbers of a people who were God's and not his own.

The reformed Gill presses the anatomy of the fall. For nine or ten months conscience lay asleep; now, the thing done, it wakes and accuses. He lays bare the several aggravations — the pride and vain confidence in the creature, the numbering undertaken without seeking God's mind and without the half-shekel ransom the law required (compare Exodus 30:12), perhaps with a design to lay a poll-tax on the people once counted. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes what distinguishes this repentance from the last: after Bathsheba, David's conscience had to be roused by Nathan's parable, but here "David's own conscience was awakened, without the necessity of being roused" by a prophet's visit — though he adds the sobering qualification that "ten months had passed before David saw his sin."

The wesleyan Clarke reaches after the inward nature of the offence, guessing that David, coveting some extension of empire, "was naturally curious to know whether the number of fighting men in his empire was sufficient for the work which he had projected"; and he draws the hard lesson that "when God is offended and refuses assistance, vain is the help of man." The witnesses do not contend; they agree that the mark of a true penitent is here — the heart smiting itself unbidden, the frank confession "I have sinned greatly," the cry for pardon, "take away the iniquity of thy servant." What Henry says of it all may stand for them together: it is well, when a man has sinned, "to have a heart within him to smite him for it."
11:
David rises in the morning to a word from God through Gad his seer, and the witnesses divide gently over the order of grace: did the reproof waken the repentance, or the repentance draw the reproof? The grammatical-historical Ellicott is emphatic. The verse should read "and when David was up," and carries "no suggestion ... that David's repentance was in consequence of the visit of Gad; on the contrary, it was in consequence of his repentance and confession that the prophet was sent to him." Ellicott adds a note on the man: Gad has not been named since he counselled David to leave the land of Moab (compare 1 Samuel 22:5), yet was probably a counsellor all along, and from a later notice it may even be that this whole account came from his pen (compare 1 Chronicles 29:29).

The reformed Gill reads the grammar the same way and to the same end. The words should be rendered "and David arose in the morning," lest they seem to imply "as if he had no sense of his sin before the prophet came to him"; whereas it was in the night that David had been under conviction, had confessed, and had prayed for pardon, "upon which the prophet was sent to acquaint him what was the will of God concerning him." The confession of the previous verse thus stands first, and Gad follows.

The wesleyan Clarke leans the other way, supposing "that David's contrition arose from the reproof given by Gad, and that in the order of time the reproof came before the confession stated in the 10th verse." He describes the seer plainly as "a holy man of God, under the Divine influence, whom David had as a domestic chaplain." The difference is one of sequence, not of substance, and it is a difference worth pausing on: whether the penitent moved first toward God or God first toward the penitent. Keil and Delitzsch simply mark that Gad came by Jehovah's command to lay the fault before David and set the coming judgment before him. All agree that the office of the prophet here is not to convict — David is already broken — but to carry back God's answer to a man who has cast himself on mercy, and to frame the strange, searching choice that follows.
12:
God's message through Gad is short and startling — "I offer thee three things; choose thee one of them" — and the witnesses, few here, dwell on the tenderness hidden in so severe an offer. The reformed Gill catches a change in the very address. Where once the Lord had bidden Nathan say "unto my servant David" when David's heart was set on building him a house (compare 2 Samuel 7:5), "now he had sinned and displeased the Lord, and therefore it is only plain David." The dropped title is a small wound, and Gill will not let it pass unnoticed: the honour of being called God's servant is for a season withheld from a servant under correction.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the offer in its frame, reading verses eleven through fourteen as one long period: the word of the Lord goes forth to Gad, Gad comes to David, and God lays before him three judgments — years of famine, months of flight before his enemies, or days of pestilence — that David may say what answer the seer shall carry back to him who sent him. The mercy, they and Gill agree, is real even in the severity: a sinner justly liable to be cut off is not simply struck, but consulted; the rod is offered to his own hand before it falls.

The two witnesses leave the reader to feel the weight of that consultation. It would have been justice to send any one of the three unasked; instead the penitent king is made to weigh them, and in the weighing to measure his sin by the dreadfulness of its wages. The very form of the offer — "that I may do it unto thee" — keeps the doing firmly in God's hand while granting David a solemn share in the choosing. Here is the first token that David has not been cast off: God still speaks to him, still by a prophet, still offering rather than merely inflicting. The plain name David, stripped of "my servant," tells him he is chastened; the very fact of the choice tells him he is not forsaken. What the previous verses established — that he had confessed and was heard — this verse confirms in an unexpected shape, that being heard does not mean escaping the rod, but bearing it from a Father's, and not an enemy's, hand.
13:
Gad comes and spreads the three judgments before the king, and the witnesses gather chiefly around a single numeral. As the text stands, the first option is "seven years of famine"; but the wesleyan Clarke and the grammatical-historical Ellicott agree, against the received reading, that the true number is three. The parallel in 1 Chronicles 21:12 reads "three years," and the Septuagint reads three here as well; Clarke explains the slip mechanically, the Hebrew letter for seven having been mistaken by a copyist for the similar letter that stands for three. Ellicott adds the argument from symmetry: three years of famine would answer far better to the "three months" of flight and the "three days" of pestilence than an isolated seven.

The reformed Gill lets the scene speak of David's frame of mind. Gad, he notes, "said nothing to him about his sin, but correction for it" — which itself confirms that David had already been made sensible of the sin before the prophet arrived; the seer comes not to accuse but to set the terms of chastisement. The three evils are of a kind that "greatly weaken and diminish a people," and each is dreadful in its own way, so that the choosing itself is a further part of the discipline, and no light thing.

Small as the textual point is, the witnesses show why it matters. To leave "seven" unexamined would be to miss the deliberate architecture of the offer — three against three against three, a descending scale of duration as the severity mounts, from the slow year-long wasting of famine to the swift three days of plague. The commentators who correct the number are not merely tidying the text; they are recovering the shape in which God framed the choice, a shape that itself preaches. And Gill's observation keeps the pastoral thread taut: the prophet's silence about the sin is a mercy, for it means the work of conviction is already done, and what remains is only for the broken king to say into whose hand he had rather fall — the question that the next verse will answer with such moving faith.
14:
Pressed to choose, David makes the great confession of the chapter — "I am in a great strait: let us fall now into the hand of the LORD; for his mercies are great: and let me not fall into the hand of man" — and here the witnesses lay down their textual tools and speak as one in admiration. The wesleyan Clarke calls the choice noble and reads David's mind through the three options. Had he chosen war, his own person was in no danger, for an ordinance already kept him from the battlefield; had he chosen famine, his own wealth would have secured him and his house; but in choosing pestilence he "showed the greatness of his mind," exposing himself "to the ravages of which himself and household were exposed equally with the meanest of his subjects."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott hears in the words the return of an older, better David: "the spirit of David in his earlier years reappears." He chooses the form of punishment "which seems to him most directly and immediately dependent upon God Himself," placing himself in God's hands rather than suffering the strokes "in which the will of man seemed to have a greater share"; and Ellicott too marks that it is the one judgment "from which his own royal position would afford him no immunity." Keil and Delitzsch read the choice the same way — pestilence "comes directly from God," whereas in flight before an enemy David would have fallen into the hands of men.

The reformed Gill notes only that David, torn between evils each so grievous and forced to answer at once, does not name his choice outright, yet "by his next words, and by the event, it seems he chose the pestilence." The witnesses converge on the heart of it: a penitent, given his dread choice, flings himself not away from God but toward him, reasoning that the very hand that smites is the hand whose mercies are great. It is the logic of faith at its purest — to prefer the direct dealing of a God he has offended over the pity of men, whose "tender mercies are cruel," and to embrace the one scourge that will spare him nothing. In choosing thus David preaches, from the depth of his fall, the doctrine that undergirds the whole chapter: that it is safer to be in the hands of the God we have wronged than in any refuge of our own devising.
15:
The pestilence falls — "from the morning even to the time appointed" — and seventy thousand die from Dan to Beersheba; the witnesses labour over the mysterious span of time and the terror of the toll. The chief question is the meaning of the phrase rendered "the time appointed." The wesleyan Clarke takes it of the three days God had proposed, though he grants that "the plague was terminated before the conclusion of the third day," so that Jerusalem was spared. The grammatical-historical Ellicott leans the other way: the Hebrew "probably means 'time of assembly,'" that is the hour of the evening sacrifice — so the Chaldee and Jerome understood it — which would compress the whole visitation into "a single day." Keil and Delitzsch reason against the three-day sense on the same ground, that the plague was stayed early because God repented of the evil (compare v.16), and that the phrase lacks the article and cannot bear the definite "the appointed time"; they record the old Greek guess "till the hour of breakfast."

The reformed Gill marks the direct hand of God in it: the Lord "directly employing an angel to go through the coasts" of the land and empowering him to inflict a pestilential disease. Ellicott too notes how the Chronicler removes the abruptness with which "the angel" enters here, telling how God sent an angel to Jerusalem to destroy it (compare 1 Chronicles 21:15), and he pauses over David's cry for the people — "these sheep, what have they done?" — observing that though David seeks to take all blame on himself, his prayer could not simply be granted, "such was the divinely ordained federal relation between the ruler and his people that they were necessarily involved in the guilt of their head."

Beneath the dispute over hours the witnesses hold one thing steadily: the stroke came straight from God, swift and sovereign, sparing neither the number David had gloried in nor the security he had trusted. Whether the plague ran three days or shrank to a single evening, its brevity is itself a mercy — the shortening of the term, as Keil and Delitzsch and Clarke both note, is God's own compassion breaking through the judgment. And Ellicott's word on the "federal relation" keeps the sober lesson in view: a king's sin does not fall on the king alone, so that the tally of the slain is at once the wages of David's pride and the sombre proof that rulers and peoples are bound together under the hand of God.
16:
The destroying angel stretches his hand over Jerusalem, and then the great reversal comes — "the LORD repented him of the evil ... It is enough: stay now thine hand" — and the witnesses fasten on the visible angel, the "repentance" of God, and the holy ground where the sword was sheathed. The reformed Gill describes the apparition: the angel stood "between the earth and the heaven," in the air right over Jerusalem, a drawn sword stretched over the city (as the Chronicler tells, compare 1 Chronicles 21:16), the whole a menace to inject terror into David and the city and to warn them of what impended. The wesleyan Clarke agrees that the angel was truly "an object of sight," seen not only by David but by Ornan and his four sons (compare 1 Chronicles 21:20), and he lingers, as commentators must, over the tangle of that owner's name, spelled Araunah, Araniah, and Ornan across the two histories.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch weigh the boldest word in the verse — that "Jehovah repented of the evil." They gather it with the like sayings of Scripture (compare Exodus 32:14; Jeremiah 26:13; and the note on Genesis 6:6): God did not change his mind but stayed the angel's hand before Jerusalem, so that the city itself was spared, and the destroyer stood arrested at the threshing-floor of Araunah the Jebusite.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the threads into worship. Perhaps there was more wickedness, and more of the pride now punished, in Jerusalem than elsewhere, and so the hand was stretched over it; "but the Lord repented him of the evil, changed not his mind, but his way." Henry seizes, with Dr. Lightfoot, the wonder of the place: on Mount Moriah, "in the very place where Abraham was stayed from slaying his son, this angel, by a like countermand, was stayed from destroying Jerusalem" — and he draws the gospel out of it, "it is for the sake of the great Sacrifice that our forfeited lives are preserved from the destroying angel." The witnesses do not dispute; together they read in the arrested sword the readiness of God to forgive, his small delight in punishing, and the mercy that, at the very site where the Temple would rise, says over a guilty city, It is enough.
17:
Seeing the angel that smote the people, David lifts up the noblest prayer of his old age — "Lo, I have sinned ... but these sheep, what have they done? let thine hand be against me, and against my father's house" — and the witnesses read it as the very pattern of a shepherd's and a penitent's heart. The reformed Gill marks first the direction of the prayer: David spoke "not unto the angel" but to Jehovah who sent him, he and the elders of Israel clothed in sackcloth and fallen on their faces. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw out its logic: "I the shepherd of Thy people have sinned and transgressed, but the nation is innocent" — innocent, that is, not of all guilt whatever, but of the particular sin God was here punishing. It belongs to the nature of true penitential prayer, they say, that a man "takes all the blame upon himself" and will not shelter his own sin behind the faults of others.

The wesleyan Clarke turns to the hard providence the verse exposes — that "in the order of Providence there is no way of punishing kings in their regal capacity, but by afflictions on their land, in which the people must necessarily suffer." He reaches for the pagan poet's line, that whatever folly the kings commit, the people are scourged for it, and he takes David's plea "against my father's house" as the offer of his own family to bear the stroke. The witnesses stand together in reverence before the scene. David, who had gloried in the greatness of his people, now begs to be himself the whole of the sacrifice; the king who counted them as his strength owns them as God's flock and himself as the guilty shepherd. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill alike hear in the prayer the mark of genuine repentance — the sinner claiming the punishment and pleading for the spared. And the older readers could not fail to see, in the shepherd offering himself that the sheep might live, a shadow of that greater Shepherd who was smitten that the flock might be spared, so that David's cry becomes, without straining, a foregleam of the cross toward which the whole chapter has been quietly moving.
18:
Gad returns that day with a word of pure grace — "Go up, rear an altar unto the LORD in the threshingfloor of Araunah the Jebusite" — and the witnesses read the command itself as the sign that God is reconciled. The puritan Matthew Henry draws out the comfort: had the Lord meant to kill David "he would not have accepted an offering, and therefore would not have ordered him to build an altar." Here is peace made between God and sinners "by sacrifice, and not otherwise, even by Christ the great propitiation, of whom all the legal sacrifices were types"; it is for his sake that the destroying angel is bidden stay his hand. Henry then follows David to the purchase and finds there both justice and humility — the king who would not so much as use the ground for his altar, though the owner was an alien and himself a king with an express command from God, until he had bought and paid for it, "for God hates robbery for burnt-offering."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that only part of God's word through Gad is given here, the rest unfolding in the story that follows, and that the altar with its burnt-offerings and peace-offerings was to appease the wrath of Jehovah so that the plague might be turned from Israel. Ellicott, with the Chronicler, adds that Gad came "by direction of the angel" (compare 1 Chronicles 21:18): David, still on the hill of Zion, had looked out and seen the angel "by the threshing-floor of Araunah," on the lower hill of Moriah — and he marks the lasting consequence, that "it was doubtless this event that determined the Temple-site."

The wesleyan Clarke enriches the same ground with older memory, naming this Mount Moriah where, the rabbins held, Cain and Abel offered, where Abraham bound Isaac, and where Solomon's temple would afterward rise. The witnesses converge on a single movement of thought: the altar is at once the token of a pardon already granted and the appointed means by which the plague is stayed, and the very floor where the sword hung is transformed into the place of sacrifice. Grace runs ahead of the offering — God commands the altar because he is already reconciled — and the spot of judgment becomes the cradle of the Temple, so that the worst day of David's later reign fixes forever the place where Israel would meet its God.
19:
The obedience is immediate — "David, according to the saying of Gad, went up as the LORD commanded" — and the witnesses, few here, dwell on the readiness of a chastened king and the meeting that follows. The reformed Gill traces the movement literally, up "from the place where he was in a lower part of the city to Mount Moriah, on which was the threshing floor, a place fit for winnowing corn when threshed." The homely detail is worth its keeping: the ground that God had chosen for his altar was a working floor, a place of labour and of the separating of grain from chaff, and to it the penitent king climbs at the prophet's bare word.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the scene forward to the encounter at the top. As David and his servants came up, Araunah looked out from the enclosure of his threshing-floor, went out to meet them, and bowed low to the earth, asking what had brought the king; and David answered plainly, "To buy the floor from thee, to build an altar to the Lord, that the plague may be turned away from the people." The whole errand is stated in a breath, and the witnesses let its directness speak.

What the two commentators leave the reader is a picture of faith in motion. There is no delay, no sending of a servant, no standing on royal dignity: the word of God through Gad is scarcely spoken before David is on the road up the hill. The obedience matches the repentance — a man truly broken does not argue the terms of his restoration but goes up at once to do the thing commanded. And Gill's note on the threshing-floor quietly deepens the moment: on a place made for winnowing, where the good grain is parted from the worthless husk, David will build the altar that turns away wrath, as if the very ground preached that judgment and mercy have met, and that what remains of Israel is the wheat God has spared from the fan. The verse is a hinge, but a telling one, showing the king who once overrode a prophet's counsel now hastening to fulfil a prophet's word.
20:
Araunah looks up and sees the king and his servants coming toward him, and the witnesses catch both the homely realism of the scene and a small textual crux. The reformed Gill fills in the human background from the Chronicler: Araunah "peeped up out of the place in which he had hid himself with his four sons, for fear of the angel" (compare 1 Chronicles 21:20). The terror of the vision still lay on the threshing-floor; the owner and his sons had gone to ground before the drawn sword of heaven, and it is out of that hiding that he now lifts his head to see, of all things, the king himself climbing the hill.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the courtesy that follows: Araunah "looked out," went forth to meet the coming party, "bowed low even to the earth," and asked what occasioned the king's visit. Ellicott adds the fine point of comparison between the histories — that here Araunah "saw the king," whereas the parallel says he saw the angel, "the words in Hebrew being much alike." It is a difference of a letter, and the two accounts stand side by side, the one telling what terrified him, the other what he now beheld.

Between the witnesses the verse becomes a study in the manners of grace. Here is a Jebusite, of the old dispossessed stock of Jerusalem, bowing to the earth before David not in dread of conquest but in simple reverence, and about to prove himself, in the verses that follow, more generous than many a son of Israel. The very abruptness with which he had hidden from the angel and now comes out to honour the king throws the two powers of the chapter into one frame: the sword that drove men to cover, and the mercy that is turning the place of terror into a place of worship. Ellicott's textual note, far from a mere scholar's quibble, marks how closely the terror and the reconciliation lie together in the sacred record — a hair's breadth in the Hebrew between seeing the destroyer and seeing the king who comes to build the altar that stays him.
21:
Araunah's question — "Wherefore is my lord the king come to his servant?" — and David's plain answer draw from the witnesses a meditation on royal humility and holy purpose. The reformed Gill hears in the Jebusite's words both wonder and welcome. There is amazement "that so great a person should visit him in his threshingfloor; that a king should come to a subject his servant, who should rather have come to him"; it is a condescension Araunah marvels at, and at the same time a courteous desire to learn the king's pleasure, since so great a man would not come in person save on some urgent errand.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give David's reply its full simplicity: "To buy the threshingfloor of thee, to build an altar unto the LORD, that the plague may be stayed from the people." Every clause of it tells — the buying, the building, the staying of the plague — and it sets the king's whole purpose before the astonished owner without a wasted word. The two witnesses leave the exchange to make its own impression, and the impression is deep. Here is a monarch who might have commandeered the ground, coming as a suppliant to a Jebusite's threshing-floor; a man under the very shadow of judgment, whose one concern is not his dignity but the ending of his people's plague. The condescension Gill marvels at is the fruit of the repentance the chapter has traced: pride brought the census and the pestilence, and now humility climbs the hill to buy a poor man's floor and rear an altar there. David's answer, so bare of self, gathers up the movement of the whole story — the king who had numbered the people that he might glory in their strength now spends himself only that the plague "may be stayed from the people," their good and not his greatness his single aim. What Araunah cannot yet see, and what the reader does, is that this small transaction on a threshing-floor is fixing the place where the God of Israel will be worshipped for ages to come.
22:
Araunah answers the king's request with open-handed generosity — "Let my lord the king take and offer up what seemeth good unto him: behold, here be oxen for burnt sacrifice, and threshing instruments ... for wood" — and the witnesses dwell on the largeness of his heart. The wesleyan Clarke reads the Jebusite's motives with warm sympathy: he "felt for the king," and showed his loyalty by the offer; he "felt for the people," willing to make any sacrifice to see the plague stayed; he "felt for his own personal safety," and gladly gave up all to save his life; and above all he "felt for the honour of God," rejoicing that he had a sacrifice to hand, "so that God might magnify both his justice and mercy." The one gift, in Clarke's reading, springs from a whole cluster of right affections at once.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks the promptness of it. Araunah, having heard David's errand, "has not a moment's hesitation"; that his threshing-floor should become the site of an altar he "at once considers as settled," and he would gladly have made the whole a gift. The reformed Gill simply gathers up the sweep of the offer — the ground to build on, the oxen for sacrifice, the wooden implements for fuel — "whatever came to hand proper to perform such service with," laid freely at the king's disposal.

The witnesses do not divide; they hold up Araunah as a pattern of cheerful giving to God. A Gentile by birth, lately in hiding from the angel, he proves readier to part with his goods for the worship of Israel's God than many an Israelite would have been. There is something almost apostolic in the picture Clarke paints — a man whose heart moves at once toward his king, his neighbours, his own soul, and his God, and finds in one act of giving a way to serve them all. And yet the very freeness of the offer sets up the tension the next verse will resolve: for David, who will not honour God with what costs him nothing, cannot simply accept so generous a gift. Araunah's liberality is genuine and is honoured, but it is about to meet a principle deeper than generosity, and the meeting will teach the abiding law of all true sacrifice.
23:
"All these things did Araunah, as a king, give unto the king" — and the witnesses divide over that curious phrase even as they admire the giver. The reformed Gill takes the words at their surface and finds in them a hint of rank: Araunah gives "as a king," which may mean he had been king of the Jebusites before Jerusalem was taken, or was of their royal line, or is so styled "because of his great liberality, having the spirit of a prince in him"; Gill even reaches for the classical parallel of Ulysses telling Antinous that one who looks like a king should give like a king.

The grammatical-historical and wesleyan readers suspect the text instead. Clarke insists there could be no king of the Jebusites on Mount Moriah, and no king in the land but David; he notes that the word "the king" is wanting in the Septuagint, Syriac, and Arabic, in several manuscripts, and in the parallel place, and judges it "very probable never made a part of the text" — perhaps we should read simply, "All these did Araunah give unto the king." Ellicott concurs that the clause should be rendered as part of Araunah's own speech — "The whole, O king, does Araunah give unto the king" — with the first "king" likely a scribal intrusion, and "give" meaning only "offer."

Keil and Delitzsch let the exchange run to its close: David would not accept the gift but said, "I will buy it of thee at a price, and will not offer burnt-offerings to the Lord my God without paying for them," and so bought floor and oxen for fifty shekels of silver — a figure they set beside the Chronicler's "six hundred shekels of gold," refusing to reconcile the two by the ratio of the metals. The debate over the title is fine, and Araunah's parting blessing, "The LORD thy God accept thee," rises above it; but under the philology the witnesses agree on the substance. Whether or not the Jebusite bore a king's name, he behaved with a king's generosity, and David, honouring that generosity, would not requite it by making God's altar cost him nothing — the very refusal that the next verse states as a law.
24:
David's answer to Araunah's generosity has become a proverb of true religion — "Nay; but I will surely buy it of thee at a price: neither will I offer burnt offerings unto the LORD my God of that which doth cost me nothing" — and the witnesses set it forth as the abiding principle of all sacrifice. The reformed Gill notes that David insisted on "a full price too," resolved to give the owner as much as the ground and oxen were worth, and to take no advantage of his liberality. The wesleyan Clarke turns the verse into a maxim: "Honour the Lord with thy substance ... He who has a religion that costs him nothing, has a religion that is worth nothing." Had Araunah's noble offer been accepted, Clarke reasons, "it would have been Araunah's sacrifice, not David's," and would not have answered the end of turning away God's displeasure — for it was David that sinned, and David that "must offer sacrifice, and at his own expense too."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott finds in the words "the principle on which David acted ... which essentially underlies all true sacrifice and all real giving to God." He then faces the arithmetic honestly. The fifty shekels of silver are said expressly to cover both the ground and the oxen, and seem a small sum; the Chronicler names "six hundred shekels of gold by weight" (compare 1 Chronicles 21:25). Ellicott reviews the ingenious attempt to harmonize the two — that the silver was really "money," reckoned in gold — but rejects it as "quite inconsistent with the text in both places," concluding that in one of them "the statement of price must have been altered in transcription," and that the uncertainty over how much of Moriah was bought makes it impossible to decide between them.

The textual difference does not touch the point, and the witnesses know it. Whether the sum was fifty shekels of silver for the floor alone or a larger price for the whole hill, the lesson David enacts is one: that what is offered to God must cost the offerer something, or it is no offering at all. Gill's insistence on the full price, Clarke's maxim, and Ellicott's principle all converge on the same refusal — a refusal to let worship be cheap. The king who had so grievously sinned will not now cut a bargain with his repentance; he pays for his altar, that the sacrifice may truly be his own, and in doing so teaches the church for ever that love which will not be at charges is not yet love.
25:
The chapter, and the book, end at the altar — "David built there an altar unto the LORD, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. So the LORD was intreated for the land, and the plague was stayed from Israel" — and the witnesses read in it the completed movement from sin to reconciliation. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the two kinds of offering, the burnt-offering and the peace-offering or shelamim (compare Judges 20:26; 1 Samuel 13:9), and observe that this remark "brings to a close not only the account of this particular occurrence, but also the book itself." They add what the Chronicler supplies beyond it: that Jehovah answered with fire from heaven upon the offering, and that David, so answered, fixed on this floor as the site of the Temple to come (compare 1 Chronicles 22:1).

The wesleyan Clarke seizes on that fire as the seal of acceptance: the sacrifices pleased the Lord, for "David called upon the LORD, and he answered him from heaven by fire upon the altar" (compare 1 Chronicles 21:26); the plague was stayed, and Jerusalem, spared, did not share the common calamity. He notes too that the book is left unfinished, its story carried on only in the Chronicler's fuller record. The reformed Gill marks simply that the altar rose "after he had made the purchase," the sacrifice following the price paid.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott gathers the whole into a closing benediction over David's life. The tabernacle and its altar stood then at Gibeon, but David dared not go there "because of the sword of the angel"; here instead the fire fell, and he declared, "This is the house of the LORD God, and this is the altar of the burnt offering for Israel." With this, Ellicott says, "David's repentance and reconciliation to God after his second great sin closes this narrative and this book" — a life that stands "a witness to all time of the power of Divine Grace over human infirmity and sin, of God's faithfulness and mercy to those that trust in Him." The witnesses end where the historian ends, at a smoking altar on Mount Moriah, the plague stayed and the wrath appeased: burnt-offerings to the glory of God's justice, peace-offerings to the glory of his mercy, and over the whole the quiet promise that the very place of judgment shall become the place where God is worshipped. As Matthew Henry had said of the altar, Christ is our sacrifice, in whom alone we escape the flaming sword and find favour with God.
